(2) Learning Hexo from Scratch - What Is Hexo?
Before we get into actually learning Hexo, let's spend a minute on what Hexo actually is and where it came from.

Hexo is a static site generator — you could also just call it a blog framework. The opposite is dynamic sites, which usually need a database behind them. WordPress is a classic example of a dynamic site.
Hexo is built on top of Node.js. A few things I personally like about it:
Compile speed is really fast — hundreds of articles take just a few seconds

My blog has about 400+ posts and Hexo reads all the files in about 6 seconds and finishes compiling in 1.83 minutes. The exact number depends on your Node.js version, Hexo version, and how many plugins you've got installed. I install a lot, so my times are a bit slower than baseline (laughs).
The official plugin list has around 349 plugins. I'll cover the must-have ones later in the series.
The official Hexo theme list has over 313 themes. I'll go through a few of them later and walk through installing and configuring them.
You can write your articles in Markdown, which is great if Markdown is your thing.
And if you want to write pure HTML, that works too — just change the file extension from
One-click deploy to GitHub Pages, Heroku, and other static hosts
Hexo has an official one-click deploy plugin.
If you're using GitHub or GitLab as your blog host, it's basically zero cost — your only real costs are images and the time to learn Hexo.
Search "Hexo 架設" or "Hexo 教學" in Chinese and you'll find a ton of tutorials.

You might be thinking "what about Hugo?" Hugo's compile speed is even faster — I haven't played with it, but I've seen benchmarks and the speed is no joke. That's a fair point. But this series is about Hexo, so if you're curious about Hugo, feel free to try it on your own. Worth noting: starting with Hexo 4, compile performance jumped a lot.
Quick history. The origin story is here: "

". The author mentions he was originally using Octopress, but Octopress was really slow to compile — 50-something articles took over a minute. Compared to my current 400+ articles, having 50 take a minute is genuinely frustrating. Plus Octopress hadn't been updated in a while at that point. So the author wrote his own wheel — Hexo. The takeaway: thanks to Octopress, we now have Hexo (lol).
If you go read that post, you'll see a bunch of people leaving pilgrimage comments at the bottom. It's from 2012, but it's only because of the author's hard work that we have today's Hexo to use.

A lot of people also recommend Hexo as a blog tool — mainly because the learning curve is relatively gentle and Chinese resources are solid. That said, you can still hit landmines along the way. For me personally, when I first set up Hexo it took me about three days to get everything working, and I tripped over plenty of stuff along the way. I was also
chatting with a friend while setting it up. (And ended up dragging a few of them into using Hexo with me.)

The current Hexo version is 4 — at the time I'm writing this, Hexo 5 just dropped. If you're thinking about jumping in to build your own blog, or you're stuck on an older Hexo version (before 4), I'd suggest upgrading to Hexo 4 or starting fresh on Hexo 4. The reason is that compile speed between Hexo 4 and Hexo 3 is genuinely a big difference. The other big factor is your Node.js version — Hexo 4 plus a suitable Node version compiles really fast. The official write-up has the test details:
Speed is the Key - How We Make Hexo 30% Faster

Compile speed will still depend on a few factors — your machine, how many plugins you have, etc. But my own tests had Hexo 4 noticeably faster than Hexo 3 (I went from about 60-second compiles down to around 15 seconds).
That wraps up today's Ironman post. Now that you know a bit of Hexo's history, next we'll install Hexo.
